original_hypothesis.title: LifiImplementation._forward Leaves Residual ERC20 Allowance to LiFi Router After Swap
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: LifiImplementation._forward sets a token allowance to the LiFi router via forceApprove but never revokes it after the swap completes. If LiFi uses less than the approved amount (partial fill), or if the swap data is crafted to not consume the full amount, a residual allowance persists allowing the LiFi router to spend remaining tokens from the LifiImplementation contract.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: GearBoxV3.executeCommands explicitly revokes approval after operations (_revokeApproval), showing the developers are aware of this pattern. LifiImplementation lacks this cleanup step. Tokens remaining in LifiImplementation after a partial swap are at risk.

proof_of_signal.pattern: _setAllowance(token, lifi, amount) called before lifi.call but no forceApprove(lifi, 0) after

preconditions: LiFi router performs a partial fill or the swap doesn't consume the full approved amount
Tokens remain in LifiImplementation after the swap
LiFi router or a LiFi-approved spender acts on the residual allowance

initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Residual allowance enables LiFi to pull additional tokens in future interactions
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Tokens left in LifiImplementation can be drained via the standing allowance

economic_questions: Can LiFi partial fills leave tokens in the LifiImplementation contract?
Does the LiFi router have any mechanism to use standing allowances?
Are tokens ever left in LifiImplementation between transactions?
validation_requirements: Test LiFi swap with partial fill to check residual balances and allowances
Verify whether tokens can accumulate in LifiImplementation
Check if rescueFunds can recover stuck tokens

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_execution_cost: Gas for triggering LiFi to use residual allowance
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Up to residual token amounts per swap

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: After any swap with partial fill
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Limited per-swap but cumulative over time

potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Cumulative residual amounts across all swaps

validation_result.mechanism: missing approval revocation after external call
validation_result.impact_summary: LifiImplementation._forward sets an ERC20 allowance to the LiFi router via forceApprove but never revokes it after lifi.call completes, leaving a residual allowance that could be used to drain tokens remaining in the contract.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The _forward function in LifiImplementation.sol first pulls tokens from the connector via sendTokensToTrustedAddress, then sets an allowance to the LiFi router via _setAllowance (which calls forceApprove), then executes lifi.call with the swap data. After the call succeeds, there is no revocation of the allowance (no forceApprove(lifi, 0)). If the LiFi router performs a partial fill or the swap data is crafted to not consume the full approved amount, a residual allowance persists on the LifiImplementation contract. Any tokens remaining in LifiImplementation are then at risk of being pulled by the LiFi router through the standing allowance. This is a well-known security anti-pattern. The developers are clearly aware of this pattern, as evidenced by GearBoxV3.executeCommands which explicitly calls _revokeApproval after its multicall operation.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: The proof_of_signal correctly identifies the _forward function as the location of the defect. The _setAllowance call at LifiImplementation.sol sets the approval, and no corresponding revocation exists after the lifi.call. GearBoxV3.sol's executeCommands demonstrates the correct pattern with _revokeApproval after the external call. The code comparison makes the defect unambiguous.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: After lifi.call completes successfully, the allowance set via _setAllowance(token, lifi, amount) is never revoked. If the LiFi router does not consume the full approved amount, a residual allowance persists, allowing the LiFi router to pull any remaining tokens from the LifiImplementation contract in future interactions.
This helper sets the allowance unconditionally. There is no corresponding _revokeAllowance or cleanup function called after the swap completes.

This is the CORRECT pattern used in GearBoxV3. After the external call (multicall), the approval is explicitly revoked via _revokeApproval. LifiImplementation._forward is missing this revocation step.

calling_sequence: 2. LifiImplementation.performSwapAction records balance before, calls _forward, records balance after
3. _forward pulls tokens from connector via sendTokensToTrustedAddress to LifiImplementation
4. _forward calls _setAllowance(token, lifi, amount) which does token.forceApprove(lifi, amount)
5. _forward calls lifi.call{value: msg.value}(data) which executes the swap
6. If swap is partial fill, LiFi consumes less than approved amount
7. Function returns without revoking the approval - residual allowance persists
8. Remaining tokens in LifiImplementation are at risk via standing allowance
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: The vulnerable path starts at performSwapAction or performBridgeAction, both of which call _forward. Inside _forward, _setAllowance(token, lifi, amount) is called before lifi.call, but no forceApprove(lifi, 0) is called after the external call succeeds. This means the LiFi router retains spending authority over any unconsumed tokens. The same flow applies to performBridgeAction which also calls _forward.

config_validation.deployment_evidence: No deployment scripts provided for LifiImplementation configuration. The constructor takes swapHandler and _lifi addresses.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: The attack scenario requires: (1) A swap or bridge operation that results in a partial fill by the LiFi router, leaving unconsumed tokens in LifiImplementation, (2) The residual allowance from the forceApprove remains active, (3) A subsequent interaction where the LiFi router (or a contract authorized by it) pulls the remaining tokens. While LiFi is a trusted router, the principle of least privilege dictates that approvals should be revoked after use. The scenario is realistic because partial fills are a normal occurrence in DeFi swaps, and the rescueFunds function can recover tokens but does NOT revoke allowances, meaning even manual recovery leaves the allowance vulnerability in place.
validation_result.impact_assessment: MEDIUM impact. The defect leaves standing ERC20 allowances to the LiFi router after every non-native token swap or bridge. If any tokens accumulate in LifiImplementation (via partial fills, dust, or direct transfers), they are at risk. The impact is bounded by: (1) the amount of tokens that accumulate in LifiImplementation between transactions, (2) the trust relationship with the LiFi router - if the router is compromised or has undiscovered vulnerabilities, accumulated tokens could be drained. The risk compounds over time as multiple swaps leave residual allowances for different tokens.
recommended_mitigations: Add token.forceApprove(lifi, 0) after the lifi.call in _forward to revoke the allowance, matching the pattern used in GearBoxV3.executeCommands
Consider using a pattern that approves only the exact amount needed and always revokes after use, e.g.: _setAllowance(token, lifi, amount); lifi.call(...); token.forceApprove(lifi, 0);
Add a _revokeAllowance helper function to LifiImplementation for consistency with BaseConnector._revokeApproval
In rescueFunds, also revoke any standing allowances to the lifi router for the rescued token
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy LifiImplementation with a mock LiFi router that consumes only 50% of approved amount. 2. Execute a swap via performSwapAction. 3. After swap completes, check IERC20(token).allowance(lifiImpl, lifi) - should show residual allowance equal to 50% of amount. 4. Verify tokens remain in LifiImplementation. 5. From the mock LiFi router, call transferFrom to pull the remaining tokens using the standing allowance. 6. Verify tokens are successfully pulled, confirming the vulnerability.
uncertainties: Whether the official LiFi diamond proxy has any mechanism that could exploit standing allowances - this depends on LiFi's internal architecture
The frequency and magnitude of partial fills in practice with LiFi swaps
Whether tokens actually accumulate in LifiImplementation between transactions in normal operation
additional_testing_needed: Test with actual LiFi router to verify partial fill behavior and residual allowance amounts
Check if LiFi router has any sweeping or transferFrom functionality that could use standing allowances
Measure typical dust/residual amounts from LiFi partial fills across various token pairs
Verify that performBridgeAction has the same vulnerability (it calls the same _forward function)
